name: RE2 and Rust regex
summary: Cox 2007 design: Thompson NFA + on-demand lazy DFA cache. Linear-time worst case (O(nm)), no backreferences or lookaround. RE2 (Google), Rust regex crate (Andrew Gallant), GNU grep all descend from this. The right choice when worst-case time matters more than syntactic features.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Russ Cox's 2007 article "Regular Expression Matching Can Be Simple And Fast" laid out the modern NFA-and-lazy-DFA regex architecture. Thompson construction builds an NFA from the regex; an on-demand lazy DFA caches subset-construction results as they're computed during matching. The result is O(nm) worst-case time — linear in input size — with no backreferences or lookaround.

The trade-off is sharp: linear worst case (no catastrophic backtracking, ever) at the cost of not supporting backreferences, lookbehind, or recursive subpatterns. For applications where the regex executes on user-controlled input at scale (log analysis, intrusion detection, source-code search), linear-time guarantees are a hard requirement.

sources.label: Cox — Regular Expression Matching Can Be Simple And Fast

sections.title: Production implementations
sections.content:
**RE2** (Google) is the canonical production implementation. Used internally at Google for many regex-on-untrusted-input workloads; open-sourced and widely adopted.

**Rust regex** (Andrew Gallant) brings the same design to the Rust ecosystem, with substantial engineering work on Unicode handling, the lazy DFA cache, SIMD prefilters, and compile-time-tunable feature sets. Rust regex is the regex engine for ripgrep, fd, and a substantial fraction of Rust ecosystem tooling. Performance is comparable to or better than PCRE-JIT on most non-pathological patterns, with the linear-time guarantee always intact.

**GNU grep** uses a similar architecture (with additional optimisations like Boyer-Moore for fixed-string fast paths). The lineage of NFA + lazy DFA dominates server-side regex applications where worst-case time matters.
